heroOverview: Osteopetrosis is a group of rare hereditary bone disorders characterised by the paradox of extremely dense but structurally fragile bone — the result of osteoclast dysfunction that prevents normal bone resorption. Rather than remodelling correctly, bone accumulates in layer upon layer until it obstructs the marrow space, narrows cranial nerve foramina, and compresses the brain stem. The condition spans a wide clinical spectrum from the autosomal recessive malignant infantile form, which carries a high risk of fatal bone marrow failure before the age of ten without a stem cell transplant, to the autosomal dominant adult form (Albers-Schönberg disease) where many individuals are only discovered incidentally on X-ray after a fracture. Worldwide prevalence is estimated at approximately 1 in 100,000 to 500,000 births depending on the subtype. Management requires a genetics-led multidisciplinary team and, for the severe infantile form, urgent haematopoietic stem cell transplantation.

keyStats.prevalence: Overall approximately 1 in 100,000 to 500,000 births; autosomal recessive infantile form approximately 1 in 250,000; autosomal dominant form approximately 1 in 20,000
keyStats.demographics: All sexes equally; autosomal recessive subtypes higher in populations with elevated consanguinity rates
keyStats.avgAge: Autosomal recessive malignant form presents in infancy; autosomal dominant form often diagnosed in adulthood after incidental radiograph or fracture
keyStats.globalCases: Rare; exact global burden unknown due to multiple subtypes and clinical spectrum

definition: Osteopetrosis (ICD-10: Q78.2) is a heterogeneous group of inherited disorders of osteoclast function in which failure of normal bone resorption leads to progressive accumulation of dense, structurally abnormal bone. In healthy bone, osteoclasts acidify a sealed resorption lacuna to dissolve mineral, then secrete proteases that degrade the organic matrix. In osteopetrosis, mutations in genes encoding components of this acidification machinery — most commonly TCIRG1 (encoding the a3 subunit of the vacuolar proton pump) in the malignant infantile form, or CLCN7 (a chloride channel) in both dominant and some recessive forms — prevent acid secretion, block resorption, and allow primary mineralised cartilage and woven bone to accumulate. The result is a skeleton that appears hyperdense on radiograph (hence the name 'marble bone disease') but has a disordered internal architecture that fractures easily. The major clinical consequences are: marrow obliteration leading to haematopoietic failure with severe anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and immunodeficiency; cranial nerve compression causing blindness, deafness, and facial palsy; and paradoxical increased fracture risk. Three broad clinical categories exist: autosomal recessive malignant infantile osteopetrosis (ARO) — the most severe; autosomal recessive intermediate form — less severe, often surviving to adulthood; and autosomal dominant osteopetrosis (ADO, Albers-Schönberg disease) — generally mild, often asymptomatic until adulthood. Rare X-linked forms also exist. Subspecialty care involves clinical genetics, haematology, and ophthalmology.

typesClassification.description: The most severe form, presenting in the first months of life with progressive bone marrow failure, hepatosplenomegaly, severe anaemia, thrombocytopenia, recurrent infections, and cranial nerve compression. Without haematopoietic stem cell transplant (HSCT), most children die before age ten from infection or haemorrhage. TCIRG1 mutations account for about 50% of cases.

Presents in childhood with fractures, anaemia, and cranial nerve involvement but without the severe bone marrow failure of ARO. Survival to adulthood is possible. Caused by mutations in CLCN7, OSTM1, or other genes.

The most common form, caused by heterozygous CLCN7 mutations. Often asymptomatic and discovered incidentally on radiograph; some individuals develop fractures, osteonecrosis of the jaw, or mild anaemia. Life expectancy is generally normal.
A rare X-linked disorder caused by IKBKG mutations combining osteopetrosis, anhidrotic ectodermal dysplasia, and immunodeficiency; affects males predominantly.

primarySymptoms: Pathological fractures despite dense bone — the abnormal bone architecture lacks normal laminar structure and breaks under forces that would not injure normal bone; fractures often occur at the femoral shaft and other long bones.

Severe anaemia from obliteration of the medullary cavity, leaving no space for haematopoiesis; the body responds with extramedullary haematopoiesis in the liver and spleen, causing hepatosplenomegaly.
Thrombocytopenia and abnormal bleeding resulting from marrow failure and platelet underproduction.
Recurrent severe infections (bacterial and fungal) secondary to neutropenia from bone marrow obliteration and intrinsic osteoclast-mediated immune signalling defects.
Progressive visual loss from optic nerve compression as bone encroaches on the optic canal; may begin with night blindness and progress to complete blindness if untreated.
Facial palsy (unilateral or bilateral) from narrowing of the facial nerve canal, resulting in inability to close the eye, asymmetric smile, and risk of corneal exposure injury.
Sensorineural and conductive hearing loss from narrowing of the internal auditory canal and ossicular involvement.
Macrocephaly and frontal bossing in infantile forms from pressure hydrocephalus and compensatory skull expansion over compliant sutures.
Dental abnormalities including delayed tooth eruption, dental crowding, and osteonecrosis of the jaw (particularly in ADO) due to poor bone turnover affecting the mandible.
Hypocalcaemia in neonates and infants with severe ARO, because the dense bone sequesters calcium and osteoclasts cannot release it from bone; causes irritability, muscle cramps, and seizures.
earlyWarningSigns: Unexplained severe anaemia in an infant that does not respond to iron supplementation.
A fracture in a baby or toddler from minimal force, with a radiograph showing abnormally white, dense bones.
Progressive visual difficulty or nystagmus in a young child with abnormally dense bones on any prior imaging.
Hepatosplenomegaly on abdominal examination in an infant with pancytopenia.
emergencySigns: Rapidly worsening vision or sudden vision loss in a child known to have osteopetrosis — optic nerve decompression within hours to days may preserve remaining sight.
Severe infection (sepsis) in an infant with ARO, which carries very high mortality given the underlying immunodeficiency.
Hypocalcaemic seizures in a neonate or infant — requires urgent intravenous calcium and specialist assessment.
Signs of brainstem compression (apnoea, bradycardia, declining consciousness) from narrowed foramen magnum requiring emergency neurosurgical assessment.

causes.description: Biallelic loss-of-function mutations in TCIRG1 eliminate the a3 subunit of the vacuolar H+-ATPase proton pump on the osteoclast ruffled border, preventing acidification of the resorption lacuna and completely blocking mineral dissolution from bone surfaces.

CLCN7 encodes the ClC-7 chloride channel that couples to the proton pump to maintain electroneutrality during acid secretion. Dominant-negative CLCN7 mutations disrupt both the mutant and the normal channel, producing ADO; biallelic null mutations cause a severe ARO phenotype.

OSTM1 encodes a beta subunit required for ClC-7 stability. Loss-of-function causes severe ARO combined with progressive neurodegeneration distinct from the purely mechanical nerve compression of other subtypes.

Mutations in TNFSF11 (RANKL) or TNFRSF11A (RANK) block the essential osteoclast differentiation signal, producing osteoclast-poor forms of osteopetrosis with a distinct radiographic pattern and a worse response to HSCT.
Biallelic mutations in CA2 affect acidification in both osteoclasts and renal tubules, producing a combined phenotype of osteopetrosis, renal tubular acidosis, and cerebral calcification.

Multiple additional genes encoding proteins involved in the osteoclast vesicular trafficking and secretory pathway have been identified; together they account for a minority of cases where TCIRG1 and CLCN7 testing is negative.

riskFactors.factor: Carrier parents for autosomal recessive subtypes
riskFactors.description: Each pregnancy of two carrier parents carries a 25% probability of a severely affected child. Consanguinity substantially increases the chance that both parents carry the same recessive allele.

First and second-degree cousin unions raise the probability of both parents carrying a rare recessive osteopetrosis mutation; the condition is disproportionately represented in communities with consanguineous marriage practices.

Autosomal dominant osteopetrosis is transmitted by an affected parent with 50% probability per pregnancy; a family history of characteristic fractures or incidental dense bone on X-ray should prompt consideration of the diagnosis.
A couple with one ARO-affected child faces a 25% recurrence risk in each subsequent pregnancy; prenatal diagnosis is available once the causative variant is identified.
Certain TCIRG1 mutations are enriched in populations including Costa Rican and some Middle Eastern communities, raising disease incidence above the global average in those groups.
Osteopetrosis is a purely genetic condition; no environmental exposures, medications, or nutritional factors are known to cause or significantly modify its development.

affectedDemographics: Male and female individuals equally across all subtypes
Infants and young children with autosomal recessive malignant infantile osteopetrosis
Adults of any age with the autosomal dominant Albers-Schönberg form, many of whom are asymptomatic
Populations with higher consanguinity rates have increased incidence of autosomal recessive subtypes
Siblings of an affected child with a confirmed recessive mutation who have not yet been tested
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of osteopetrosis begins with the characteristic radiographic finding of uniformly increased bone density affecting the entire skeleton. Classic radiographic signs include endobone (bone-within-bone) appearance in the vertebrae and long bones, alternating dense and lucent bands at the metaphyses ('sandwich vertebrae'), and dense skull base with narrowed foramina. These findings may be incidental in ADO or may be sought following an atypical fracture, unexplained anaemia, or cranial nerve palsy. Full blood count with differential detects the pancytopenia, and a peripheral blood smear may show erythrocyte precursors (leukoerythroblastic picture) from bone marrow infiltration. Bone marrow biopsy confirms marrow obliteration. Serum markers of bone turnover show suppressed resorption markers with normal or elevated formation markers. CT and MRI of the skull and spine define cranial nerve canal narrowing, degree of stenosis at the foramen magnum, and any hydrocephalus. Molecular genetic testing — a skeletal dysplasia panel or sequencing of TCIRG1, CLCN7, OSTM1, SNX10, RANKL, and RANK — confirms the causative mutation and guides prognosis and treatment planning. Parental testing to confirm carrier status is performed when a recessive diagnosis is established. Ophthalmological and audiological assessment is performed at diagnosis and at regular intervals thereafter.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the characteristic diffuse bone density, endobone sign, sandwich vertebrae, and dense skull base that are pathognomonic of osteopetrosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A series of plain X-rays of skull, spine, pelvis, and long bones; painless, takes 15–20 minutes. The finding is usually striking even to non-specialist radiologists.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and peripheral smear
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the degree of anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and neutropenia from marrow failure; the smear detects the leukoerythroblastic pattern characteristic of marrow infiltration.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within a few hours. Serial counts monitor disease progression and response to treatment.

diagnosticTests.test: CT scan of skull, orbits, and temporal bones
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures optic canal and facial nerve canal dimensions and identifies foraminal narrowing to guide timing of decompressive surgery and degree of cranial nerve risk.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 5–10 minute scan without contrast for bone detail; slight radiation exposure. Children may require sedation.

diagnosticTests.test: Molecular genetic panel (TCIRG1, CLCN7, OSTM1, others)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative mutation, classifies the subtype, predicts prognosis, guides transplant planning, and enables carrier testing and prenatal diagnosis in the family.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood draw or cheek swab; sequencing results typically take two to six weeks from a clinical molecular genetics laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms marrow obliteration by bone and absence of haematopoietic space; also identifies whether osteoclasts are present (osteoclast-rich forms) or absent (RANKL/RANK mutations), which affects transplant candidacy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A needle biopsy under local anaesthesia or sedation from the posterior iliac crest; results from the haematopathologist take three to five days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Suppressed TRAP5b (osteoclast activity marker) and CTXI (resorption marker) with normal or elevated P1NP (formation marker) supports the diagnosis biochemically.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within 24–48 hours from most laboratories.

diagnosticTests.test: Ophthalmological assessment with visual evoked potentials
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies optic nerve function and identifies subclinical optic atrophy before irreversible visual loss occurs; guides urgency of optic canal decompression.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A combination of slit-lamp examination, visual field testing (where developmentally feasible), and VEP where electrodes on the scalp measure optic nerve conduction.

treatmentOverview: The only curative treatment for autosomal recessive malignant infantile osteopetrosis is allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT). Engraftment of donor osteoclast precursors allows functional osteoclasts to develop in the recipient, restoring bone resorption. Results are best when transplantation is performed before the age of three to five years, before irreversible cranial nerve damage, and before severe neurological compromise from OSTM1-related neurodegeneration. HLA-matched sibling donors provide the best outcomes (approximately 75–85% long-term survival), and matched unrelated donor transplants are increasingly successful. For the milder autosomal dominant and intermediate forms, treatment is largely symptomatic: fracture management by orthopaedic surgeons familiar with the abnormal bone biology, monitoring and decompression of cranial nerve canals, audiological management, and dental care. Calcitriol (activated vitamin D) combined with a calcium-restricted diet has been used in some centres to attempt to stimulate residual osteoclast activity by increasing calcium flux; results are inconsistent. Interferon-gamma has shown modest haematological benefit in some ARO patients awaiting transplant. Supportive therapy includes red cell and platelet transfusions, G-CSF for neutropenia, and prophylactic antimicrobials. Genetic counselling and prenatal diagnosis for future pregnancies are integral to management.

medicalTreatments.name: Haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT)
medicalTreatments.description: The only proven cure for malignant infantile ARO. Donor myeloid precursors differentiate into functional osteoclasts that can resorb bone, reversing marrow obliteration and haematopoietic failure. Preferably performed before age three with an HLA-matched donor.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term survival approximately 75–85% with HLA-matched sibling donors; approximately 60–70% with matched unrelated donors in published series. Optic nerve damage that has already occurred is not reversed.

medicalTreatments.name: Calcitriol (1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D3) therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Supraphysiological doses of calcitriol with dietary calcium restriction are used in some centres to stimulate osteoclast activity in residual osteoclasts; most useful in less severe forms.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Evidence is inconsistent; some case series report modest haematological improvement and slowed disease progression in intermediate forms, but no randomised trials support this as standard care.

medicalTreatments.name: Interferon-gamma 1b
medicalTreatments.description: Subcutaneous injections of recombinant interferon-gamma activate macrophages and may stimulate osteoclast function in osteoclast-rich forms; sometimes used as bridging therapy before transplant.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Haematological improvement reported in approximately 30–40% of treated patients in case series; does not substitute for HSCT in severe ARO.

medicalTreatments.name: Transfusion support (RBC and platelets)
medicalTreatments.description: Red blood cell transfusions address symptomatic anaemia; platelet transfusions reduce bleeding risk in thrombocytopenic patients. Used as bridge to HSCT or long-term supportive care in non-transplant candidates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for acute symptom management; does not address the underlying disease and carries risks of alloimmunisation over time.

medicalTreatments.name: G-CSF (granulocyte-colony stimulating factor)
medicalTreatments.description: Stimulates neutrophil production from residual marrow or extramedullary sites, reducing infection risk in neutropenic patients with ARO.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severe infection episodes in approximately 50% of neutropenic ARO patients in published case series.

surgicalOptions.name: Optic canal decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Endoscopic or transcranial surgical enlargement of the optic canal to relieve pressure on the optic nerve and preserve or recover vision; indicated when visual evoked potentials or visual acuity is declining.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual stabilisation achieved in approximately 60–70% of cases when performed before complete atrophy; reversal of established blindness is rarely possible.

surgicalOptions.name: Foramen magnum and posterior fossa decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Neurosurgical removal of dense bone from the posterior fossa to relieve brainstem and upper cervical cord compression; indicated in patients with signs of cervicomedullary compromise.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological stabilisation in approximately 70–80% of operated patients; outcomes depend on degree of pre-surgical neurological injury.

surgicalOptions.name: Orthopaedic fracture fixation
surgicalOptions.description: Pathological fractures — most commonly femoral — require specialist orthopaedic fixation; union is delayed compared with normal bone, and rigid fixation such as intramedullary nailing is challenging due to narrow medullary canals.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Fracture union is eventually achieved in most cases but delayed; reoperation rates are higher than in normal bone due to hardware failure in dense brittle bone.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary calcium restriction with calcitriol
alternativeTreatments.description: Reducing dietary calcium while providing calcitriol attempts to drive calcium mobilisation from bone through the parathyroid axis, secondarily stimulating osteoclasts; not evidence-based but used empirically in some milder cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and rehabilitation
alternativeTreatments.description: Post-fracture rehabilitation and physiotherapy address the functional limitations from abnormal gait, fracture healing, and muscle weakness, particularly in the adult dominant form.

whySeeSpecialist: A clinical geneticist is essential to classify the subtype, identify the causative mutation, and counsel the family on recurrence risk and transplant options. A haematologist with bone marrow transplant experience should be involved immediately in all infantile cases, as the transplant window of opportunity is narrow. Ophthalmology, neurosurgery, ENT, and orthopaedics provide the monitoring and intervention needed to prevent irreversible end-organ damage. General paediatricians should not manage severe osteopetrosis without specialist input.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a paediatric haematology-oncology centre with an active HSCT programme that has experience transplanting children with metabolic bone disease. The centre should have close links to paediatric neurosurgery, ophthalmology with experience in optic canal decompression, and clinical genetics with access to TCIRG1 and CLCN7 sequencing. For adults with the dominant form, a metabolic bone disease or endocrinology clinic with radiological expertise is appropriate.
preventionStrategies: Carrier testing of both parents following the birth of an affected child to confirm recessive status and counsel about 25% recurrence risk.
Prenatal diagnosis by CVS or amniocentesis in subsequent pregnancies once the causative variant is identified.
Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-M) for carrier couples who choose IVF.
Newborn screening for dense bone on plain radiograph when a sibling is affected or parental carrier status is known.
Early ophthalmological and neurological surveillance from diagnosis to identify cranial nerve compression before irreversible damage occurs.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid high-impact activities and contact sports in severe osteopetrosis due to the elevated fracture risk despite dense bone.
Maintain excellent oral hygiene and attend regular dental care, as osteonecrosis of the jaw is a recognised complication particularly in the dominant form and in bisphosphonate-treated patients.
Ensure dietary calcium intake is appropriate — neither excessively high (risks further sequestration) nor deficient (risks hypocalcaemia).
In adults with ADO, report any new jaw pain, numbness, or non-healing dental extraction sites promptly as these may indicate early osteonecrosis.
Monitor vision and hearing annually and report any change promptly, as early decompression offers the best chance of preservation.

recommended: Vitamin D supplementation as advised by the treating specialist to support immune function without over-driving calcium sequestration
Adequate protein intake to support post-surgical recovery and immune function
High-fibre diet to prevent constipation, which may be exacerbated by immobility after fractures
avoid: Excessive dietary calcium in severe ARO patients receiving calcitriol therapy, as calcium excess can worsen bone sclerosis
Raw and unpasteurised foods in severely immunocompromised ARO patients awaiting or post-transplant

exerciseGuidelines: Patients with severe ARO have severely restricted activity during periods of haematopoietic failure and post-transplant. As engraftment is confirmed and counts recover, gentle physiotherapy and progressive activity are encouraged under supervision. Adults with autosomal dominant osteopetrosis can usually exercise normally but should avoid high-impact and collision activities that carry fracture risk. Physiotherapy-guided strengthening is beneficial to maintain muscle mass and joint function during the prolonged recovery from fractures.
dailyManagement: Take all prescribed medications (calcitriol, antibiotics, antifungals) consistently and report side effects promptly.
Track transfusion dates, blood count trends, and any new symptoms for the haematology team.
Attend all scheduled ophthalmology appointments; report any change in vision immediately.
Report new jaw pain, dental problems, or healing difficulties to both the dentist and the specialist team.
After HSCT, adhere strictly to the infection prevention regimen and attend scheduled engraftment monitoring appointments.
Keep emergency contact details for the specialist team readily accessible, especially during the high-infection-risk period.
prognosis: Prognosis varies dramatically by subtype. Autosomal recessive malignant infantile osteopetrosis without HSCT has a median survival of under ten years, most children dying from infection, haemorrhage, or respiratory failure. With successful HSCT before age three, long-term survival of 70–85% is achievable, though cranial nerve damage present before transplant may not reverse. Pre-existing blindness carries a high likelihood of permanence. The autosomal recessive intermediate form has a more variable course; many affected individuals survive to adulthood but face recurrent fractures, cranial nerve complications, and progressive anaemia. Autosomal dominant Albers-Schönberg disease generally carries a normal or near-normal life expectancy; the main risks are fractures, osteonecrosis of the jaw, and occasional anaemia. OSTM1-related ARO has the worst prognosis of all, with progressive neurodegeneration proceeding independently of transplant, and most affected children die within the first decade.
recoveryTimeline: Following successful HSCT for ARO, engraftment is typically confirmed within three to four weeks. Haematological improvement — rising neutrophil counts and resolution of pancytopenia — proceeds over two to six months. Radiological improvement in bone density progresses slowly over one to three years as donor-derived osteoclasts begin resorption. Cranial nerve compression that was present before transplant may partially improve over months if the nerve is not yet atrophic, but complete recovery is uncertain. Post-transplant monitoring continues for at least five years for relapse, graft failure, and late complications. For fractures in ADO, healing takes 1.5–2 times longer than in normal bone, and rehabilitation to full function typically requires three to six months per fracture.
complications: Irreversible blindness from untreated optic nerve compression — the single most feared complication in ARO, and a key driver of urgent HSCT timing.
Fatal or severe infection from neutropenia and immunodeficiency in ARO — the leading cause of death before transplantation.
Severe anaemia and thrombocytopenia requiring chronic transfusion support, with associated risks of alloimmunisation and iron overload.
Pathological fractures, predominantly of the femur, from structurally defective dense bone; union is delayed and reoperation rates are elevated.
Osteonecrosis of the jaw in adults with ADO, particularly following dental extractions; management requires oral and maxillofacial surgery.
Progressive sensorineural and conductive hearing loss from temporal bone involvement, potentially leading to profound deafness if unmonitored.

supportResources.name: Osteopetrosis Support Trust (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing peer support, information, and advocacy for families affected by all forms of osteopetrosis.

supportResources.name: NORD — Osteopetrosis
supportResources.description: Patient-facing disease overview from the U.S. National Organization for Rare Disorders, including clinical summary and research links.

supportResources.name: NIH MedlinePlus — Osteopetrosis
supportResources.description: Plain-English genetics summary from the U.S. National Library of Medicine.

sources.title: Sobacchi C et al. Osteopetrosis: genetics, treatment and new insights into osteoclast function. Nat Rev Endocrinol, 2013
Steward CG. Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation for osteopetrosis. Pediatr Clin North Am, 2010
Orchard PJ et al. Hematopoietic cell transplantation for infantile osteopetrosis. Bone Marrow Transplant, 2015
Whyte MP. Rare Metabolic Bone Disorders: Osteopetrosis. In: Favus MJ (ed). Primer on the Metabolic Bone Diseases and Disorders of Mineral Metabolism, ASBMR

confusedWithConditions.name: Achondrogenesis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achondrogenesis causes absent or greatly reduced bone formation; osteopetrosis causes excessive bone accumulation. Both can show dense-appearing bones but by completely opposite mechanisms.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteogenesis imperfecta
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause fragile bones and fractures, but osteogenesis imperfecta produces thin, under-mineralised bone from collagen defects; osteopetrosis produces dense, over-accumulated bone from resorption failure. Radiographs are opposite in appearance.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can cause anaemia, thrombocytopenia, hepatosplenomegaly, and leukoerythroblastic blood picture, mimicking each other in infants. Bone radiograph showing diffuse sclerosis distinguishes osteopetrosis; leukaemia shows normal or lytic bone pattern.

relatedConditions.name: Achondroplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are hereditary skeletal dysplasias managed in genetics and skeletal dysplasia multidisciplinary clinics, though their mechanisms — bone elongation failure versus bone resorption failure — are distinct.

relatedConditions.name: Osteogenesis imperfecta
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the clinical problem of pathological fractures and is in the differential diagnosis when a child presents with fractures; distinguished by bone density pattern on radiograph.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A clinical near-opposite — low bone density versus high bone density — but both result in elevated fracture risk, illustrating that bone density and bone quality are not synonymous.

relatedConditions.name: Anaemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Marrow obliteration in ARO causes severe refractory anaemia; haematological management and the decision to proceed to HSCT depend on the severity of anaemia and its trajectory.

faqs.question: What is osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Osteopetrosis is a rare hereditary condition where bones become extremely dense because osteoclast cells fail to resorb old bone normally. Despite appearing marble-like on X-ray, the bone is fragile and breaks easily. Severe forms cause bone marrow failure and require a stem cell transplant.

faqs.question: Why is osteopetrosis called marble bone disease?
faqs.answer: The bones appear pure white and uniformly dense on X-ray, resembling marble. This comes from the accumulation of bone that is never resorbed, but unlike marble the internal structure is disordered, making bones brittle and prone to fracture.

faqs.question: Is osteopetrosis lethal?
faqs.answer: It depends on the subtype. The autosomal recessive malignant infantile form is fatal within the first decade without a stem cell transplant. The autosomal dominant form in adults is much milder and usually allows a near-normal lifespan.

faqs.question: What causes osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Mutations in genes essential for osteoclast acid secretion — most commonly TCIRG1 or CLCN7 — prevent osteoclasts from dissolving bone mineral. Without normal resorption, old bone accumulates and fills the marrow space.

faqs.question: How is osteopetrosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: The diagnosis is usually made from characteristic X-ray findings showing diffuse bone sclerosis, endobone appearance, and sandwich vertebrae. Blood tests reveal anaemia and low platelet counts from marrow failure. Molecular genetic testing identifies the causative mutation.

faqs.question: Can osteopetrosis be cured?
faqs.answer: The severe infantile form can be cured by allogeneic haematopoietic stem cell transplantation, which replaces defective osteoclast precursors with functioning donor cells. Other forms have no cure but are managed with monitoring and surgery for complications.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for infantile osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Haematopoietic stem cell transplantation from an HLA-matched donor is the only curative treatment for severe infantile osteopetrosis. It should be performed before age three to five for best results. Supportive treatment includes transfusions, antibiotics, and interferon-gamma while awaiting transplant.

faqs.question: Does osteopetrosis cause blindness?
faqs.answer: Yes, in severe forms. Dense bone gradually narrows the optic canal and compresses the optic nerve, leading to progressive vision loss and potentially permanent blindness. Early surgical decompression of the optic canal may preserve vision if performed before the nerve is severely damaged.

faqs.question: Why do dense bones fracture in osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Although the bone is dense, it lacks the organised lamellar structure of normal bone. The accumulated unmineralised cartilage and woven bone are architecturally weak, similar to chalk — dense but brittle — so fractures occur at lower forces than in normal bone.

faqs.question: Is osteopetrosis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes. Infantile forms are autosomal recessive — two carrier parents each have a 25% risk of having an affected child. The adult Albers-Schönberg form is autosomal dominant — an affected parent has a 50% transmission risk. Genetic testing of the family is important.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence risk in future pregnancies?
faqs.answer: For autosomal recessive forms, confirmed carrier parents have a 25% risk per pregnancy. For autosomal dominant forms, an affected parent has 50% risk per pregnancy. Prenatal diagnosis and preimplantation genetic testing are available once the causative variant is identified.

faqs.question: How does osteopetrosis affect the blood?
faqs.answer: Dense bone fills the marrow cavity, leaving no space for blood cell production. The result is severe anaemia, low platelets causing bleeding, and neutropenia causing vulnerability to infection. The body attempts to compensate by producing blood cells in the liver and spleen, causing them to enlarge.

faqs.question: What is the Albers-Schönberg form of osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Albers-Schönberg disease is the autosomal dominant form caused by CLCN7 mutations. It is much milder than infantile forms and is often discovered incidentally on X-ray after a fracture or routine imaging. Most affected individuals have normal blood counts and near-normal life expectancy.

faqs.question: Why does osteopetrosis cause anaemia?
faqs.answer: Bone fills the medullary cavity where blood cells are normally produced, leaving insufficient space for haematopoiesis. As marrow space disappears, the body shifts blood cell production to the liver and spleen (extramedullary haematopoiesis), but this cannot maintain adequate cell production and anaemia results.

faqs.question: Can hearing loss from osteopetrosis be treated?
faqs.answer: Conductive hearing loss from narrowed ear canals can sometimes be improved by surgical decompression, though hearing aids are often needed. Sensorineural loss from cochlear or nerve damage is harder to treat. Regular audiological monitoring from diagnosis allows early intervention.

faqs.question: What dental problems does osteopetrosis cause?
faqs.answer: Teeth may erupt late and be crowded due to dense jaw bone. The major dental risk in the adult dominant form is osteonecrosis of the jaw, particularly after tooth extraction, where blood supply cannot penetrate the dense bone adequately to heal the extraction socket. Preventive dental care and avoiding unnecessary extractions are important.

faqs.question: Is gene therapy being developed for osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Yes, gene therapy using viral vectors to correct TCIRG1 mutations in haematopoietic stem cells is in preclinical and early clinical development. Early results are promising and may eventually offer an alternative or supplement to allogeneic HSCT, avoiding graft-versus-host disease risk.

faqs.question: What should I watch for if my child has osteopetrosis?
faqs.answer: Report any change in vision immediately to the specialist team. Unexplained fever, unusual bleeding or bruising, sudden weakness of the face, and any bone pain after a minor injury all warrant urgent review. Regular blood count monitoring and imaging surveillance are essential.

faqs.question: What happens if osteopetrosis is not treated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, severe infantile osteopetrosis leads to progressive bone marrow failure, severe anaemia, fatal infections, and blindness, with death typically before age ten. Milder forms lead to recurrent fractures, hearing loss, and cranial nerve palsies with diminished quality of life.

faqs.question: Are fractures common in adults with Albers-Schönberg disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. Despite dense bones, fractures are more frequent than in the general population, affecting roughly 70% of individuals with ADO at some point. The femur and vertebrae are common sites. Fracture healing is delayed, and specialist orthopaedic input from surgeons experienced in osteopetrosis is important.

metaDescription: Osteopetrosis causes dense, fragile 'marble bones' from osteoclast failure, leading to fractures and marrow loss. Learn subtypes, transplant treatment, and care.
